summary:
Gilgal is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic A site in the lower Jordan Valley, near the modern city of Jericho in the West Bank, occupied from approximately 9650–9550 BCE. It is one of several small PPNA villages in the Jordan Valley and is significant primarily for its exceptionally early evidence of fig cultivation — analysis of charred fig remains from Gilgal suggests that the inhabitants were cultivating parthenocarpic (seedless) fig trees, which cannot reproduce without human intervention and therefore constitute direct evidence of deliberate cultivation.

If the dates and botanical interpretation for the Gilgal figs are confirmed, fig cultivation at Gilgal would represent the world's earliest known example of fruit tree cultivation — predating olive, grape, and date cultivation by several thousand years and providing evidence that the earliest Neolithic farmers in the Jordan Valley were deliberately managing tree crops alongside cereal grains. Gilgal thus contributes important evidence to the understanding of the broad range of plants being brought under cultivation during the [[event:1304|Pre-Pottery Neolithic A]] period in the Levant, suggesting that the agricultural revolution was more diverse and multifaceted than a simple focus on wheat and barley might imply.
